inlineContent.text: Search-as-you-type is a great user experience, but firing a network
request on every keystroke wastes bandwidth and hammers your server.
A debounced pipeline collapses rapid keystrokes into a single request
that fires only after the user pauses. FlowKit makes this a four-operator
so it can flow through FlowKit operators without
type signals that network calls can fail.
pipeline maps everything into this one type so views never
deal with raw arrays or raw errors.
The repository is the boundary between the pipeline and the
network. Defining a clean protocol lets you swap in a stub during
tests without changing any pipeline code.

Write a test that sends three rapid keystrokes within the
300 ms window, then asserts no value has arrived yet. After

pipeline must never terminate on a network error.
lets tests advance virtual time instead of sleeping.
cancels the previous inner flow to avoid
delivering stale search results from a superseded query.
“To avoid delivering stale results from a superseded query.”
When the user types a new character, the previous network
it immediately so only the most recent query’s results
“To reduce memory usage by removing old flows from the heap.”
Memory is reclaimed as a side effect, but the primary
reason is correctness: an old request might resolve after
a newer one, showing the user stale results.

A five-second stop timeout keeps the upstream alive
“It immediately cancels the upstream network request.”
A five-second grace period covers SwiftUI view rebuilds
and short navigation round-trips without tearing down and
“It waits five seconds before stopping the upstream, so a
new subscriber arriving quickly gets the live stream.”
controls when the upstream stops, not
how long values are replayed. Use
“It replays the last five seconds of emitted values.”
the last SwiftUI subscriber navigates away?

abstract.text: Authentication state is the classic cross-cutting concern: the home
tab, the profile screen, and a UIKit sign-in controller all need to
react when the user signs in or out. Traditional approaches like singletons
a reactive stream every layer of your app can observe independently.
values through delegate methods or completion handlers rather than
the rest of your app can work with a clean, cancellable stream.
Real sleeps make tests slow and flaky. FlowKit ships a
virtual clock, and a suite of
helpers that let you assert on stream values
Learn FlowKit by building real features. These tutorials walk through
the patterns that come up most often: search-as-you-type, shared state
across screens, wrapping callback APIs, and writing deterministic tests.

content: // Use TestClock to advance virtual time. No real sleeps, no flaky CI.
// Rapid keystrokes, each within the debounce window
// No results yet because the debounce window hasn't closed
// Advance past the 300 ms debounce window
// Now the pipeline fires exactly once with the latest query

content: // Expose a hot StateFlow so every subscriber gets the current value
// immediately. The pipeline stops collecting 5 seconds after the last
// subscriber leaves, keeping the subscription alive across brief
return ThrowingFlow { collector in try await collector.emit(.idle) }
await collector.emit(.error("Search failed. Please try again."))

// @CollectedState observes the hot StateFlow and re-renders the view
// whenever SearchState changes. The initial value matches the flow's
// initialValue so there's no flash on first render.
description: Text("Type to find products in the catalog.")
